enonce.texte: This exercise evaluates your understanding of vocabulary related to academic performance. Read each definition or context carefully and choose the correct word or expression.

questions.texte: Identify the word that corresponds to this definition: 'A formal test of knowledge or ability in a particular subject, usually taken at the end of a term or academic year.'

sousQuestions.texte: Which of the following is a synonym for a less formal 'exam'? (a) Prize (b) Quiz (c) Scholarship

questions.texte: Analyze the sentence: 'After receiving an **outstanding** grade in Mathematics, she was awarded a **scholarship**.' What does the word 'outstanding' mean in this context?

sousQuestions.texte: Based on the same sentence, what is the primary benefit of a 'scholarship'? (a) A free laptop (b) Financial aid for studies (c) A summer job

questions.texte: Complete the following analogy: 'Success' is to 'Failure' as 'Prize' is to:

enonce.texte:
Read the case study below about a student named Alice and answer the questions that follow, applying the vocabulary and concepts of academic performance.

Case Study: Alice is a Première student in Yaoundé. She wants to obtain a scholarship to study Medicine at the University of Yaoundé I. Her first term score was 12/20. She needs a minimum grade of 16/20 in Science subjects to be eligible. Her teacher told her that her performance was 'good but not outstanding.' For the second term, she set a goal: get a prize for being the best student in Biology. She decided to stop watching TV and focus on her exam preparation.

questions.texte: List two academic performance indicators mentioned in the case study that are used to measure Alice's success.
Differentiate between the terms 'score' and 'grade' as used in the Cameroonian educational context, based on the text.
questions.reponse: In this context, 'score' refers to the numerical mark obtained (e.g., 12/20), while 'grade' can refer to a required level or standard (e.g., needing a minimum grade of 16/20) or a letter/number category. They are closely related but 'grade' can be a threshold or category.

questions.texte: Judge Alice's strategy for improving her academic performance. Is simply 'stopping watching TV' sufficient to achieve an 'outstanding' grade? Justify your answer using the vocabulary of the lesson.
questions.reponse: No, it is likely not sufficient. While removing distractions is a good start, achieving an 'outstanding' grade (16/20+) and winning a 'prize' requires a more comprehensive strategy. She needs to include active study methods, seek help, practice past 'exam' papers, and manage her time effectively. 'Stopping TV' is a passive change, not an active learning strategy.

intitule: Grammar in Context: Present Perfect & Past Simple (Review)

enonce.texte:
Complete the following conversation between two students about their academic results. Use the correct tense: Present Perfect or Past Simple. The verbs are in parentheses.

Student A: "I am so happy! I ______ (just / receive) my test results. I ______ (get) 18/20 in English!"

Student B: "That's fantastic! I ______ (never / score) that high. Last term, I ______ (have) 14/20, but this time I ______ (work) very hard and I ______ (improve) to 15/20."

Student A: "Congratulations! ______ you ______ (see) the list of scholarship winners yet?"

Student B: "No, I ______ (check) it yesterday, but it wasn't there. They probably ______ (post) it this morning."

questions.texte: Conjugate the verbs in parentheses to complete the dialogue.
questions.reponse: have just received; got; have never scored; had; worked; have improved; Have you seen; checked; have posted

enonce.texte: You are a student advisor at a high school in Douala. A group of students in Première come to you for advice. They want to apply for a competitive scholarship, but their academic performance is mixed. Use your knowledge of the lesson's vocabulary and grammar to help them.

questions.texte: Evaluate the situation: One student says, "I have a good 'score' in every subject, but my overall 'grade' is not 'outstanding'." What is the difference between a good 'score' in each subject and an 'outstanding' overall 'grade'?
questions.reponse: A good 'score' in each subject might mean passing marks (e.g., 12/20), but an 'outstanding' overall 'grade' would require consistently high marks (e.g., 16/20 or above) in almost all subjects. The average is key.

questions.texte: Formulate a three-step action plan for a student who has never won a 'prize' or 'scholarship' but wants to do so next term. Use at least three words from the lesson (exam, score, success, etc.).
questions.reponse: 1. **Analyze past performance:** Review your last 'exam' papers to identify weak areas. 2. **Set a target 'score':** Aim for a specific, high 'score' (e.g., 17/20) in each subject. 3. **Define 'success' metrics:** Break down the 'grade' requirements for the scholarship and create a weekly study plan to meet them.
